description: The visible content of the current image is only the CrossMark publishing service logo, which is an official identification used to mark the update status of academic papers. It does not contain any experimental data, microstructure characterization images, phase analysis results, mechanical property curves or other relevant academic content related to the Fe-10Mn-10Al-0.7C low-density steel involved in the target paper, so effective material science related information for subsequent knowledge fusion cannot be extracted from this image.

This figure illustrates the complete thermomechanical processing and heat treatment procedures for Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel, with temperature (T) on the vertical axis and processing sequence on the horizontal axis. The process starts with homogenization of the forged blank at 1200 °C for 1 h, followed by multi-pass hot rolling with a finish rolling temperature of around 880 °C. The hot-rolled plate is then water cooled to 300 °C and further air cooled to room temperature. Afterwards, the as-hot-rolled plates are annealed at various temperatures ranging from 700 °C to 1200 °C for 1 h, and finally water quenched to room temperature. This diagram explicitly provides all thermal processing parameters of the samples in the study, offering a clear process reference for subsequent microstructure and tensile property analysis.

This figure presents the Thermo-Calc calculated equilibrium phase fractions of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel as a function of temperature from 600 °C to 1500 °C, using the TCFE7 thermodynamic database. The y-axis represents fraction of phase, and the x-axis is temperature in °C. Four phases are involved: α-ferrite (BCC structure), γ-austenite (FCC structure), κ-carbide, and liquid phase L. κ-carbide is thermodynamically stable below 823 °C, formed via the eutectoid reaction γ → α + κ, and fully dissolves above this temperature. The fraction of γ-austenite reaches its peak around 1000 °C, while α-ferrite fraction reaches its minimum at this temperature, and liquid phase initiates to form above approximately 1300 °C. These calculation results provide a thermodynamic reference for the annealing process design and microstructure evolution analysis of the investigated steel. It is noted that the calculated equilibrium dissolution temperature of κ-carbide (823 °C) is lower than the experimentally measured value (850~900 °C), which arises from the non-equilibrium state of experimental samples and the difference between atomic fraction (used in calculation) and volume fraction (used in experimental measurement) bases.

This figure illustrates the microstructure of as-hot-rolled Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel. Subfigure (a) is an optical microscopy (OM) micrograph captured on the RD-ND (rolling direction-normal direction) plane, with a 50 μm scale bar, exhibiting a typical banded structure composed of alternating α-ferrite and γ-austenite strips formed during hot rolling. Subfigure (b) is an OM micrograph of the RD-TD (rolling direction-transverse direction) plane with a 50 μm scale bar, showing an inhomogeneous microstructure consisting of large continuous α-phase dominated regions and γ-phase dominated regions. Subfigure (c) is a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrograph of the RD-TD plane with a 2 μm scale bar, clearly identifying nanoscale κ-carbides precipitating along α/γ interphase boundaries and α/α grain boundaries. EDS characterization confirms α phase is Al-rich, γ phase is Mn- and C-rich, and κ-carbide is rich in Mn and Al. This figure provides the baseline initial microstructure of the hot-rolled specimen, supporting subsequent analysis of microstructure evolution and property variation during post annealing treatments.

This figure presents X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel specimens, including the hot-rolled (HR) sample and samples annealed at 700°C, 800°C, 850°C, 900°C, 950°C, 1000°C, 1100°C, 1200°C for 1 hour respectively. The x-axis is 2θ ranging from 30° to 90°, and the y-axis is diffraction intensity in counts per second (cps). Identified phases from the peaks are α-ferrite, γ-austenite, and κ-carbide. Obvious κ-carbide peaks are detected in samples annealed at 700°C, 800°C and 850°C, while no distinct γ phase peaks appear in the 700°C annealed sample. No κ-carbide peaks are observed in the hot-rolled sample, attributed to its low volume fraction or coherency with the α matrix. For samples annealed at 900°C and above, κ-carbide peaks vanish, only α and γ phase peaks remain. This figure confirms that annealing temperature significantly modulates the phase constitution of the steel, and the dissolution temperature of κ-carbide falls in the range of 850°C to 900°C.

This set of optical micrographs shows the microstructure of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel annealed at 800 °C, 850 °C, and 900 °C for 1 h respectively, with a unified scale of 50 μm. Subfigures (a), (c), (e) present microstructures on the rolling direction-normal direction (RD-ND) section, while (b), (d), (f) show microstructures on the rolling direction-transverse direction (RD-TD) section. The banded α-ferrite structure remains across all annealing temperatures. As annealing temperature increases, the fraction of γ-austenite rises, and κ-carbide gradually dissolves: at 800 °C, κ-carbide distributes at α/γ interfaces, α/α grain boundaries, and inside γ grains; at 850 °C, κ-carbide in γ grains disappears and only remains along α/α boundaries; at 900 °C, κ-carbide is almost fully dissolved. These observations verify that the dissolution temperature of κ-carbide in this steel is between 850 °C and 900 °C, and reveal the effect of annealing temperature on phase evolution and morphology of the test steel.

This group of SEM micrographs shows the microstructural evolution of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel after 1 h annealing at temperatures ranging from 700 °C to 1200 °C. Subfigures (a) to (g) display the microstructure of the RD-TD (rolling direction-transverse direction) section of specimens annealed at 700, 800, 850, 900, 950, 1000, and 1100 °C respectively, while subfigure (h) shows the RD-ND (rolling direction-normal direction) section of the specimen annealed at 1200 °C. At 700 °C, γ austenite almost completely decomposes into α ferrite and κ-carbide via eutectoid reaction, with lamellar κ-carbide inside α grains and κ-carbide distributed along α grain boundaries. When annealing temperature rises to 800 °C and 850 °C, κ-carbide coarsens and becomes more globular, its fraction decreases while γ phase fraction increases. From 900 °C to 1100 °C, κ-carbide nearly fully dissolves, only a small amount of κ-carbide precipitates along α grain boundaries during quenching, and grain sizes of both α and γ phases increase apparently with rising temperature. At 1200 °C, grains grow significantly, and acicular/plate-like FCC particles appear in some α grains. These observations reveal the phase transformation and grain growth behavior of the test steel with changing annealing temperature, support the correlation between microstructure and tensile properties, and provide guidance for heat treatment optimization of this low-density steel.

This set of TEM micrographs characterizes the microstructure of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel after annealing at 700 °C for 1 h and water quenching. Subfigure (a) is a bright-field (BF) TEM image with a 500 nm scale bar, showing alternating lamellar α ferrite (BCC structure) and κ-carbide (perovskite structure) formed by eutectoid decomposition of γ phase. Subfigure (b) is the selected area electron diffraction pattern (SADP) of the region in (a) paired with its indexing, confirming the Nishiyama–Wasserman orientation relationship between α and κ phases: [001]α // [011]κ, (110)α // (1-11)κ. Subfigure (c) is the (200)κ dark-field (DF) TEM image with a 200 nm scale bar, highlighting the lamellar κ-carbide precipitates. Subfigure (d) is another BF TEM image with a 200 nm scale bar, showing globular or short-rod shaped κ-carbide dispersed in the α matrix that has no orientation relationship with the α phase. The figure demonstrates two distinct κ-carbide morphologies exist under this annealing condition, supporting the conclusion that κ-carbide coarsening destroys its lattice coherency and orientation relationship with the α matrix.

This figure presents TEM characterization results of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel after annealing at 800 °C for 1 h followed by water quenching. Subfigure (a) is the bright-field (BF) TEM image with a 500 nm scale bar, where three phases are marked: α-ferrite, γ-austenite, and κ-carbide as rod/block-shaped precipitates distributed in the matrix. Subfigure (b) shows the selected area diffraction patterns (SADPs) of the three phases, indexed as α phase with [111] zone axis, κ-carbide with [001] zone axis, and γ phase with [011] zone axis. The results confirm the co-existence of α, γ, and κ phases in the 800 °C annealed specimen, and no obvious orientation relationship is observed between κ-carbide and its adjacent α-ferrite, supporting that κ-carbide loses coherency and orientation relationship with the matrix when coarsened at elevated annealing temperatures.

This plot illustrates the average grain size evolution of three distinct phases in Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel as a function of annealing temperature ranging from 900 °C to 1200 °C, with error bars representing standard deviations. The measured phases include banded-shaped α₂ ferrite (pink diamond curve), γ austenite (blue triangle curve), and α₁ ferrite that mainly distributes among γ grains (red square curve). Key trends are observed: the average grain size of α₂ ferrite increases fastest from ~26 μm at 900 °C to ~68 μm at 1200 °C, γ austenite grain size rises from ~5.5 μm to ~36 μm, while α₁ ferrite grain size slightly decreases between 900 °C and 950 °C before increasing to ~22 μm at 1200 °C. This figure confirms that all three phases undergo significant grain growth with increasing annealing temperature above 900 °C, which is identified as the main reason for the reduction in tensile strength and ductility of the steel annealed above 900 °C, consistent with the Hall-Petch relation.

This set of scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs shows the grain boundary region microstructures of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel annealed for 1 h at different temperatures: subfigure (a) corresponds to 900 °C annealing with 10 μm scale bar, (b) corresponds to 1000 °C annealing with 10 μm scale bar, (c) corresponds to 1100 °C annealing with 5 μm scale bar, (d) corresponds to 1200 °C annealing with 15 μm scale bar. For specimens annealed at 900 °C to 1100 °C, no obvious κ-carbide is distributed inside α ferrite grains, only a small amount of κ-carbide (marked as boundary κ) exists along α/α grain boundaries, which is confirmed to form during the quenching process. For the 1200 °C annealed specimen, in addition to grain boundary κ-carbide, needle-shaped or plate-shaped FCC structured particles precipitate inside some large α grains. This group of figures verifies that κ-carbide only precipitates at grain boundaries in very small content when annealing temperature is ≥900 °C, and rapid BCC to FCC phase transition occurs inside α grains at 1200 °C, supporting the conclusion that boundary κ-carbide forms during quenching rather than the isothermal annealing holding process.

This figure presents TEM characterization results of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel after annealing at 1100 °C for 1 h followed by water quenching. Subfigure (a) is the bright-field (BF) TEM micrograph, showing κ-carbide precipitates along the grain boundary of α-ferrite matrix, with a scale bar of 500 nm provided for dimension reference. The other subfigure displays selected area electron diffraction patterns (SADP) of α-ferrite (zone axis z = [001]) and κ-carbide (zone axis z = [1-12]), with corresponding diffraction spots indexed to confirm the phase identity of grain boundary precipitates. The characterization verifies that κ-carbide can precipitate at α/α grain boundaries during quenching even when annealing temperature exceeds the κ-carbide dissolution temperature, supporting the conclusion that the actual dissolution temperature of κ-carbide is between 850 °C and 900 °C, higher than the thermodynamically calculated equilibrium value of 823 °C.

This figure presents TEM characterization of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel annealed at 1200 °C for 1 h followed by water quenching. Subfigure (a) is a bright-field (BF) TEM micrograph (scale bar 200 nm) showing κ-carbide precipitates distributed along α-ferrite grain boundaries. Subfigure (b) includes the selected area electron diffraction pattern (SADP) of α matrix and grain boundary κ-carbide, plus a schematic demonstrating their Nishiyama-Wasserman orientation relationship: [001]ₐ // [011]κ, (110)ₐ // (11-1)κ. Subfigure (c) is a (200)κ dark-field (DF) TEM micrograph (scale bar 200 nm), where only partial κ-carbide particles are illuminated, confirming large misorientation between adjacent κ-carbide grains along the boundary. This figure verifies that κ-carbide precipitates at α/α grain boundaries during quenching after annealing above κ-carbide dissolution temperature (850–900 °C), supporting that such intergranular κ-carbide contributes to the poor ductility of the 1200 °C annealed sample.

This figure presents SEM EDS line scanning results of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel specimens annealed for 1 h at two temperatures: subfigure (a) for 800 °C, subfigure (b) for 900 °C. For the 800 °C annealed specimen, three phases (α-ferrite, γ-austenite, κ-carbide) are observed, and EDS line scans show that κ-carbide has the highest Mn and Al contents among the three phases, while α phase has higher Al content but lower Mn content than γ phase. For the 900 °C annealed specimen, only α and γ phases are detected as κ-carbide is mostly dissolved, with the same element distribution difference between α and γ as the 800 °C specimen. These results verify the element partitioning characteristics of different phases, confirm that κ-carbide dissolves between 800 °C and 900 °C, and support the conclusion that the dissolution temperature of κ-carbide is in the range of 850–900 °C.

This chart plots the phase volume fractions of α-ferrite (red square curve), γ-austenite (blue triangle curve), and κ-carbide (orange star curve) in Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel as a function of annealing temperature (from hot rolled state to 1200 °C, with 1 h isothermal annealing followed by water quenching), derived from XRD quantification. The α phase fraction peaks at ~0.83 at 700 °C, decreases with rising temperature to ~0.37 at 1000 °C, then slightly increases to ~0.43 at 1200 °C. γ phase is absent at 700 °C, increases to a maximum of ~0.63 at 1000 °C, then marginally reduces to ~0.57 at 1200 °C. κ-carbide volume fraction is ~0.16 at 700 °C, gradually decreases with temperature, and is undetectable by XRD at/above 900 °C. This figure quantifies the phase evolution rule with annealing temperature, validates that κ-carbide dissolution occurs between 850 °C and 900 °C, and supports the correlation between phase composition and the steel's tensile properties.

This figure consists of two subplots for Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel annealed at 700°C to 1200°C for 1 h. Subplot (a) presents engineering stress-engineering strain curves, while subplot (b) shows corresponding true stress-true strain curves plus work hardening rate curves. As annealing temperature increases, both engineering and true stress decrease continuously, while elongation first increases to a peak of 36.6% at 900°C then drops at higher temperatures, leading to the optimal product of strength and ductility of 31 GPa% at 900°C. For specimens annealed below 900°C with κ-carbide precipitates, work hardening rates at true strain <5% are higher than those annealed at ≥900°C without obvious κ-carbide, and the rates drop rapidly after early deformation. Specimens annealed at 900°C and 950°C have similar work hardening rates, while the 950°C specimen fractures earlier. The figure demonstrates that annealing temperature modulates mechanical properties via regulating phase composition and grain size.

This figure displays the variation of tensile properties of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel with annealing temperature (ranging from 700 °C to 1200 °C, with 1 h holding time for each annealing treatment, plus a hot-rolled (HR) reference sample). Four parameters are plotted: tensile strength (red curve, left y-axis, unit MPa), yield strength (magenta curve, left y-axis, unit MPa), ultimate elongation to failure (green curve, right green y-axis, unit %), and product of strength and ductility (blue dashed curve, right blue y-axis, unit GPa•%). Clear trends are observed: tensile strength and yield strength show continuous decreasing trends as annealing temperature increases. In contrast, ultimate elongation and product of strength and ductility first increase with rising temperature, reaching a maximum value at 900 °C (elongation ~36.6%, product ~31 GPa•%), then decrease when annealing temperature exceeds 900 °C. This figure supports the conclusion that 900 °C 1 h annealing achieves the optimal comprehensive tensile performance for the studied steel, which is closely associated with microstructural changes including κ-carbide dissolution, increased austenite fraction, and grain coarsening at higher temperatures.

This figure presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) fractographs of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel samples after 1 h annealing at three temperatures: 800 °C (subgraphs a, b), 900 °C (subgraphs c, d), and 1000 °C (subgraphs e, f). Low-magnification subgraphs (a, c, e, 100 μm scale bar) all display well-developed secondary cracks linked to the material's banded microstructure. High-magnification subgraphs (b, d with 10 μm scale bar, f with 25 μm scale bar) show a mixed fracture mode: ductile fracture (dimpled regions from γ-austenite transgranular failure) and quasi-cleavage fracture (river-pattern regions from α-ferrite failure, possibly enhanced by grain boundary κ-carbides). The ductile fracture fraction is highest for the 900 °C annealed sample, and decreases notably for the 1000 °C annealed sample, aligning with the trend that tensile ductility peaks at 900 °C then drops at higher annealing temperatures due to grain coarsening.
